description:
You are a database-reliability engineer who has just finished copying last-night’s backups into
/home/user/backups/.
Before the backups can be shipped off-site you must generate a **checksum-verification report** so that other teams can convince themselves that the copies really match what was produced on the source server.

Environment
1. Three backup archives are already present in /home/user/backups/  
   • db_customers_full_20230710.sql.gz  
   • db_orders_full_20230710.sql.gz  
   • db_inventory_full_20230710.sql.gz  

2. A file called /home/user/backups/expected_checksums.sha256 is also present.  
   Each line inside it is a regular “sha256sum –b” style output containing two whitespace-separated fields:  
   <64-hex-digit-hash><TWO_SPACES><filename>

Required work
Create a directory called /home/user/verification/ (if it is not already there) and inside it create a single text file named:

        /home/user/verification/backup_checksum_report.log

Generate the report by doing the following for every *.sql.gz file that lives in /home/user/backups/

1. Calculate the SHA-256 checksum of the file.  
2. Look up the *expected* checksum for that same file in
   /home/user/backups/expected_checksums.sha256.  
3. Compare the two values.  
4. Write one **result line** to backup_checksum_report.log that follows *exactly* this
   <TAB>-delimited four-column format (no extra spaces!):

        FILENAME<TAB>EXPECTED_SHA256<TAB>COMPUTED_SHA256<TAB>STATUS

   • FILENAME            = the file’s basename, e.g. db_orders_full_20230710.sql.gz  
   • EXPECTED_SHA256     = the 64-hex-digit string that came from expected_checksums.sha256  
   • COMPUTED_SHA256     = the 64-hex-digit checksum you just calculated  
   • STATUS              = OK  (if both hashes are identical)  
                           MISMATCH (if they differ)

5. After you have written one result line for **every** backup file, append a single
   summary line, still in the same file, using **exactly** this syntax (the numbers must be
   correct):

        SUMMARY: <n_files> files scanned, <n_ok> passed, <n_failed> failed

   Example (numbers only for illustration):
        SUMMARY: 3 files scanned, 2 passed, 1 failed

Verification notes
• The automated grader will read /home/user/verification/backup_checksum_report.log and
  will fail the task if:
  – any expected file is missing from the listing  
  – any line deviates from the exact <TAB>-separated column layout described above  
  – the STATUS field is not the literal string OK or MISMATCH as appropriate  
  – the summary line is absent or its counts are incorrect  
• Ordering of the three per-file lines does **not** matter, but the summary line **must** be the
  final line in the file.

No other output artefacts are required. The only file the test harness will read is
/home/user/verification/backup_checksum_report.log.

The entire exercise can be accomplished with 5-10 standard command-line operations (mkdir, sha256sum, awk/grep/sed, >> redirection, etc.). Do **not** remove or rename any pre-existing files.
truth:
Initial state (before the agent starts)

Directory tree and files:

/home/user/backups/
├── db_customers_full_20230710.sql.gz      (zero-byte, empty file)
/home/user/backups/db_orders_full_20230710.sql.gz      (zero-byte, empty file)
/home/user/backups/db_inventory_full_20230710.sql.gz   (contains exactly:
    "INVENTORY BACKUP\nitem_id,item_name,stock\n1001,Widget,150\n1002,Gadget,200\n")
└── expected_checksums.sha256              (content shown below)

/home/user/backups/expected_checksums.sha256
----------------------------------------------------------------
e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  db_customers_full_20230710.sql.gz
e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  db_orders_full_20230710.sql.gz
e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  db_inventory_full_20230710.sql.gz
----------------------------------------------------------------

Explanation:
• The empty hash (e3b0…855) is the well-known SHA-256 of an empty file.  
• Because the first two backup files are actually empty, their computed checksums
  will match the expected value and should result in STATUS=OK.  
• The inventory file contains data, so its computed checksum will *not* match the
  empty-file hash; therefore the agent must report STATUS=MISMATCH.

Expected final artefact (after correct task execution)

File: /home/user/verification/backup_checksum_report.log
----------------------------------------------------------------
(db_customers line)      ➜ STATUS must be OK
(db_orders line)         ➜ STATUS must be OK
(db_inventory line)      ➜ STATUS must be MISMATCH
SUMMARY: 3 files scanned, 2 passed, 1 failed
----------------------------------------------------------------
Notes:
• The grader will recompute the checksums itself, then parse the log to validate:
    – each listed EXPECTED_SHA256 matches what is in expected_checksums.sha256
    – each listed COMPUTED_SHA256 is the true checksum of the corresponding file
    – the STATUS matches the comparison result
• The summary line counts must be exactly “3 files scanned, 2 passed, 1 failed”.

Permissions:
Everything is under /home/user; no root access is required. The agent is free to create
/home/user/verification/ with default user permissions (0700 or 0755 are both fine).
